---
title: "AP1_A2_TRAN Ngoc Hieu Van_22205175"
author: "Tran Ngoc Hieu Van - 22205175"
date: "2026-07-23"
output:
  pdf_document: default
---

# Introduction

This report analyzes historical data of FIFA World Cup using R Studio. The analysis includes data visualization, summary statistics, investigation on distribution of home penalty, home team scores, and match results across several factors, and filter out top 5 teams in penalty shootout matches.

Load all the dataset
```{r}
matches <- read.csv("Matches.csv")
stadiums <- read.csv("Stadiums.csv")
teams <- read.csv("Teams.csv")
tournaments <- read.csv("Tournaments.csv")
```

# Question 1

## Inspect the data structure

View the structure of each dataset
```{r}
str(matches)
str(stadiums)
str(teams)
str(tournaments)
```

Display the first six observations to quickly view the layout of each dataset
```{r}
head(matches)
head(stadiums)
head(teams)
head(tournaments)
```

Run summary to present the class and statistical distribution of every column in each dataset
```{r}
summary(matches)
summary(stadiums)
summary(teams)
summary(tournaments)
```

## Replace the missing values as "?" with "NA"
```{r}
matches[matches == "?"] <- NA # Replace the "?" values with "NA"
str(matches) # Check if the missing values "?" are replaced with NA
```

## Convert categorical variables to factors
```{r}
matches$Result <- as.factor(matches$Result)
matches$Stage <- as.factor(matches$Stage)
stadiums$Country <- as.factor(stadiums$Country)
matches$ExtraTime <- as.factor(matches$ExtraTime)
```

## Convert penalties to numeric format
```{r}
matches$HomePenalty <- as.numeric(matches$HomePenalty)
matches$AwayPenalty <- as.numeric(matches$AwayPenalty)
```

## Replace the missing value in penalties with 0
```{r}
matches$HomePenalty[is.na(matches$HomePenalty)] <- 0
matches$AwayPenalty[is.na(matches$AwayPenalty)] <- 0
```

Validate the pre-processing results
```{r}
colSums(is.na(matches)) # Check remaining missing values
str(matches) # Check variable types in the matches dataset
str(stadiums) # Check variable types in the stadium dataset
sum(is.na(matches$HomePenalty)) # Confirm no missing value in HomePenalty
sum(is.na(matches$AwayPenalty)) # Confirm no missing value in AwayPenalty
```

## Visualize the distribution of HomePenalty for matches that involved a penalty shootout

Filter matches with a penalty shootout
```{r}
library(dplyr) # Use the dplyr library for filtering
unique(matches$PenaltyShootout) # Check the value of PenaltyShootout
penalty_matches <- matches %>%
  filter(PenaltyShootout == 1) # Filter out the penalty shootout matches
penalty_matches # Check if the penalty_matches is filtered correctly
```

Since HomePenalty is a quantitative numeric variable, a histogram is selected for analyzing its distribution across matches involving a penalty shootout.
```{r}
table(penalty_matches$HomePenalty)
library(ggplot2) # Use the ggplot2 library to produces the graphic with better customization
ggplot(penalty_matches, aes(x = HomePenalty)) + 
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "lightblue", color = "black") + # Draw a histogram
  labs(title = "Distribution of Home Penalty Goals in Penalty Shootout Matches", # Add a title
       x = "Number of Home Penalty Goals", # Add label for x-axis
       y = "Number of Matches") + # Add label for y-axis
  theme_minimal() # Apply a minimal theme for a clean and simple appearance
```

**Interpretation:** This histogram indicates that most home teams score 3 or 4 penalty goals during the penalty shootouts. In contrast, lower scores (0-2 goals) and the maximum observed score (5 goals) occurred less often, suggesting that home teams generally centered around a moderate number of penalties during shootouts.

# Question 2

TournamentName is stored in the tournaments dataset, while HomeTeamScore and Stage variables are stored in the matches dataset. Therefore, the two datasets are joined using TournamentID to analyse HomeTeamScore across different tournaments.
```{r}
matches_tournaments <- matches %>%
  left_join(tournaments, by = "TournamentID") # Join TournamentName
str(matches_tournaments) # Validate if the TournamentName join successfully
```

## Create histogram to analyze the distribution of HomeTeamScore across different competition stages
```{r}
ggplot(matches, aes(x = HomeTeamScore)) + 
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "lightyellow", color = "black") + # Draw a histogram
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, max(matches$HomeTeamScore), by = 1)) + # Display integer goals
  facet_wrap(~ Stage, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) + # Compare across stages
  labs(title = "Distribution of Home Team Scores across Competition Stages", # Add a title
       x = "Number of Goals Scored by the Home Team", # Add label for x-axis
       y = "Number of Matches") + # Add label for y-axis
  theme_minimal() # Apply a minimal theme for a clean and simple appearance
```

**Interpretation:** The overall distribution of home team scores across competition stages is mainly concentrated around 1-2 scores. Since the Group Stage contains the largest number of matches, it shows the widespread of scores and a right-skewed distribution, indicating high-score performances from home team occurred occasionally. In contrast, the knockout stages, particularly final and final round, contain fewer matches and their distributions more concentrated at lower scores from the home teams. Additionally, across the remaining knockout stages, most home teams scored between 1 and 3 goals, while scoring 5 goals or more are relatively uncommon.

## Create histogram to analyze the distribution of HomeTeamScore across different tournaments
```{r}
ggplot(matches_tournaments, aes(x = HomeTeamScore)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "lightpink", color = "black") + # Draw a histogram
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, max(matches$HomeTeamScore), by = 1)) + # Display integer goals
  facet_wrap(~ TournamentName, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4) + # Compare across tournaments
  labs(title = "Distribution of Home Team Scores across FIFA World Cup Tournaments", # Add a title
       x = "Number of Goals Scored by the Home Team", # Add label for x-axis
       y = "Number of Matches") + # Add label for y-axis
  theme_minimal() # Apply a minimal theme for a clean and simple appearance
```

**Interpretation:** Overall, the distribution of home team scores across FIFA World Cup tournaments is concentrated between 0 and 3 goals, with most matches recording 1 to 2 goals. Additionally, later FIFA World Cup tournaments, particularly from 1998 onwards, contain more matches that earlier editions, resulting in taller histograms while maintaining similar home team scoring distribution. Although a few tournaments include matches where the home scored six or more goals, these high-scoring matches occurred only occasionally.

## Create histogram to analyze the distribution of HomeTeamScore across AwayTeamScore groups

Categorize the AwayTeamScore across following groups: 0-1 goals, 2-3 goals, and 4 or more goals
```{r}
matches <- matches %>%
  mutate(AwayScoreGroup = case_when( # Add a new column based on conditions applied to AwayTeamScore
    AwayTeamScore <= 1 ~ "0-1 goals", # Assign if the away team scored 0-1 goals
    AwayTeamScore > 1 & AwayTeamScore <= 3 ~ "2-3 goals", # Assign if the away team scored 2-3 goals
    AwayTeamScore > 3 ~ "4+ goals" # Assign if the away team scored more than 3 goals
  ))
```

Convert AwayScoreGroup to an ordered factor for consistent group ordering in plots
```{r}
matches$AwayScoreGroup <- factor (
  matches$AwayScoreGroup, # Specify the AwayScoreGroup variable to convert
  levels = c("0-1 goals", "2-3 goals", "4+ goals") # Define the order of the factor levels
)
table(matches$AwayScoreGroup) # Validate the grouping of AwayScoreGroup before visualizing
```

Create histogram to analyze the distribution of HomeTeamScore across AwayTeamScore groups
```{r}
ggplot(matches, aes(x = HomeTeamScore)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "lightgreen", color = "black") + # Draw a histogram
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, max(matches$HomeTeamScore), by = 1)) + # Display integer goals
  facet_wrap(~ AwayScoreGroup, scales = "free_y") + # Compare across Away Team Score groups
  labs(title = "Distribution of Home Team Scores by Away Team Score groups", # # Add a title
       x = "Number of Goals Scored by the Home Team", # Add label for x-axis
       y = "Number of Matches") + # Add label for y-axis
  theme_minimal() # Apply a minimal theme for a clean and simple appearance
```

**Interpretation:** The distribution of goals scored by Home Team is similar across the three Away Team Score groups, with most matches concentrated between 0 and 3 home goals regardless of the away team's scoring groups. From the distribution, while the 0-1 goals contains significant number of observations, the 4+ goals contains fewer observations, indicating that matches in which the away team scores 4 or more goals are relatively uncommon. Moreover, although there are few matches recorded five or more goals, these high-scoring goals are occasionally distributed across different groups of away team score.

# Question 3

The matches with a penalty shootout have already been filtered out in Question 1
```{r}
print(penalty_matches)
```

## Top 5 Home Teams in Penalty Shootouts
```{r}
topHomePenalty <- penalty_matches %>% # Count the number of matches each home team participated in
  group_by(HomeTeamID) %>% # Group all matches by the HomeTeamID
  summarize(NumberOfMatches = n(), .groups = "drop") %>% # Count the number of matches
  left_join(teams, by = c("HomeTeamID" = "TeamID")) %>% # Join the two datasets
  select(TeamName, TeamCode, NumberOfMatches) %>% # Keep only required columns
  arrange(desc(NumberOfMatches)) %>% # Sort teams from the highest to the lowest number of matches
  slice_head(n = 5) # Return only the top 5 home teams
topHomePenalty # Display the top 5 home teams in penalty shootouts
```

## Top 5 Away Teams in Penalty Shootouts
```{r}
topAwayPenalty <- penalty_matches %>% # Count the number of matches each away team participated in
  group_by(AwayTeamID) %>% # Group all matches by the AwayTeamID
  summarize(NumberOfMatches = n(), .groups = "drop") %>% # Count the number of matches
  left_join(teams, by = c("AwayTeamID" = "TeamID")) %>% # Join the two datasets
  select(TeamName, TeamCode, NumberOfMatches) %>% # Keep only required columns
  arrange(desc(NumberOfMatches)) %>% # Sort teams from the highest to the lowest number of matches
  slice_head(n = 5) # Return only the top 5 away teams
topAwayPenalty # Display the top 5 away teams in penalty shootouts
```

**Discussion:** The results indicate that Argentina, Brazil, Spain, the Netherlands, and West Germany were the top 5 home teams participated in FIFA World Cup matches decided by penalty shootouts. On the away team side, France, Argentina, England, Croatia, and Italy were the top five. There is different between the rankings of home teams and away teams, with only Argentina appearing on both lists. Viewing the two lists, France recorded the highest number of participation of an away team in penalty matches with 5 matches, while Argentina, Brazil, and Spain shared the highest number of home appearance with each participate 4 matches. Therefore, the top national teams participating in matches with penalty shootouts were not identical when home and away matches were considered separately.

# Question 4

While there are many factors influence the match outcome (Result) in the FIFA World Cup, this assignment will examine the influence of Stage and ExtraTime for the following analysis.

## Factor 1: Stage

Create table to present the influence of Stage on Result
```{r}
stage_result_table <- table(matches$Stage, matches$Result) # Create frequency table
stage_result_table # View the table
round(prop.table(stage_result_table, 1) * 100, 1) # View the table with percentage
```

Create a proportional stacked bar chart to compare match results across stages
```{r}
ggplot(matches, aes(x = Stage, fill = Result)) + 
  geom_bar(position = "fill") + # Draw stacked bars scaled to proportions (each bar sums to 100%)
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) + # Display the y-axis as percentages
  labs(title = "Match Result Distribution by Competition Stage", # Add a title
       x = "Competition Stage", # Add label for x-axis
       y = "Percentage of Matches", # Add label for y-axis
       fill = "Result") + # Set the title of the legend
  theme_minimal() + # Apply a minimal theme for a clean and simple appearance
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) # Rotate the x-axis labels
```

**Discussion on findings:** The distribution of match result varies across different competition stages. Home team win accounts for largest proportion of results in every stage. The final round and the Round of 16 show the highest proportion of home team victories, with home team winning more than third quarters of the number of matches. In contrast, the Group Stage and Second Group Stage have a noticeably higher proportions of draws more than knockout stages, indicating that draw matches are commonly occur in the beginning of the tournament. Away team has a consistent proportion across stages, accounting for around one-quarter to one-third of matches in a tournament. To conclude, competition stage is associated with changes of match outcomes with a reduced frequency of draws in knockout rounds.

## Factor 2: Extra Time

Create table to present the influence of Extra Time on Result
```{r}
extratime_result_table <- table(matches$ExtraTime, matches$Result) # Create frequency table
extratime_result_table # View the table
round(prop.table(extratime_result_table, 1) * 100, 1) # View the table with percentage to support
```

Create a proportional stacked bar chart to compare match results by Extra Time
```{r}
ggplot(matches, aes(x = ExtraTime, fill = Result)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") + # Draw stacked bars scaled to proportions (each bar sums to 100%)
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) + # Display the y-axis as percentage
  labs(title = "Match Result Distribution by Extra Time", # Add a title
       x = "Extra Time", # Add label for x-axis
       y = "Percentage of Matches", # Add label for y-axis
       fill = "Result") + # Set the title of the legend
  theme_minimal() # Apply a minimal theme for a clean and simple appearance
```

**Discussion on findings:** The distribution of match outcomes has slightly difference depending on the match proceed extra time or not. Matches with extra time recorded a lower proportion of draw and a higher proportion of away team wins compared with matches within regular time. Additionally, the proportion of home team win is generally similar between two groups, making up more than half of all matches in both case. In conclusion, the difference is modest, indicating that while extra time influences match results home team victories remain nearly the same whether extra time is proceeded.

# Question 5

## Create a GitHub repository with all relevant files supporting this analysis
The GitHub repository link: https://github.com/hieuvantnt-del/AP1_Assignment-2_TRANNgocHieuVan_22205175

## History of creating git repository and committing each question (screenshot)

### Initial Git setup on Terminal

![Display and list all the files in the current working directory. It includes A2_RScript.R file and all relevant datasets](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/All files in the folder.png)
pwd: Display the current working directory.

ls: List all files in the current directory.

![Initialise a new Git repository](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/Initialize a Git repository.png)
git init: Initialise a new Git repository.

![Stage all project files for the initial commit and check the Git status](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/Stage all project files and check the status.png)
git add.: Stage all project files for the initial commit.

git status: Check the repository status after modifying files.

### Initial commit of all the files in the working directory

![Staged all the modified files in the working directory and create initial commit](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/Create the first commit and save the staged files.png)
git commit -m: Commit the initial datasets and Rscript file with.

### Connect the local respository to the GitHub repository

![Connect the local repository to the GitHub repository and push the local repository to GitHub](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/Connect the local repository to the GitHub repository.png)
git remote -v: Show the configured remote repositories.

git remote and origin: Connect the local repository to the GitHub repository.

git branch -M main: Rename the default branch to main.

git push -u origin main: Push the local repository to GitHub and set the upstream branch.

### Question 1

![Commit and push the completed Question 1 to the GitHub](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/Commit and push the completed Q1.png)
git add: Stage the A2_RScript.R file for the second commit.

git commit -m: Commit the the completed Question 1.

git push: Push the second commit to GitHub.

### Question 2

![Commit and push the completed Question 2 to the GitHub](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/Commit and push the completed Q2.png)
git add: Stage the A2_RScript.R file for the third commit.

git commit -m: Commit the the completed Question 2.

git push: Push the third commit to GitHub.

### Question 3

![Commit and push the completed Question 3 to the GitHub](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/Commit and push the completed Q3.png)
git add: Stage the A2_RScript.R file for the fourth commit.

git commit -m: Commit the the completed Question 2.

git push: Push the fourth commit to GitHub.

### Question 4

![Commit and push the completed Question 4 to the GitHub](/Users/tranngochieuvan/AP1_Assignment2/Git Screenshot/Commit and push the completed Q4.png)
git add: Stage the A2_RScript.R file for the fifth commit.

git commit -m: Commit the the completed Question 2.

git push: Push the fifth commit to GitHub.
